topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printed summary landed on my desk with a soft, damning thud. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was a complete mismatch. The diagnoses, the treatment plans, the dates—they belonged to someone else entirely. Sarah’s meticulous, perfectly organized file had been swapped with mine. I picked up the paper again, tracing the unfamiliar handwriting detailing procedures I’d never undergone. A slow, burning heat rose up my neck. I leaned back in my chair, staring at the data, a strange, hollow feeling settling in my chest. I started tapping my pen rapidly against the desk, the sound sharp in the quiet office, trying to shake off the sudden, unwelcome shift in my internal balance.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." I whispered to the empty hallway, my voice thin and shaky. The documents spread across the sterile white surface felt like a deliberate slight. When I finally processed the sheer disparity—the superior treatment noted in the other patient’s file, the seemingly less severe prognosis—a distinct tightness constricted my throat. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the fine strands stand up on my scalp. I picked up my phone, my fingers hovering over the contact list, but the urge to call anyone felt misplaced, as if the energy needed to express this feeling was simply draining away. I just stood there, breathing shallowly, watching the fluorescent lights hum overhead, a deep dissatisfaction settling into my posture.

[story 3]

When Dr. Evans finally explained the clerical error, the words washed over me, but they didn't settle. I stared at the paper, my gaze lingering on the section detailing the successful recovery of the other individual. A peculiar restlessness began to bubble up inside me, a restless energy that made my hands twitch. I found myself compulsively re-reading the sections, searching for any flaw in the transcription, as if finding an error in the paperwork might somehow correct the imbalance I suddenly perceived. A small, involuntary sigh escaped my lips. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small office, my footsteps echoing loudly on the linoleum floor, unable to sit still with the quietude that had previously defined my routine.
